items.question_text: When a STAR or RNAV STAR is assigned, what must the clearance include?

choices.text: The name of the STAR and the transition, if necessary.
Only the destination airport.
Only the runway number.
Only the EFC time.

items.explanation: When a STAR or RNAV STAR is assigned, the clearance includes the procedure name and transition if needed.

items.question_text: If the runway assignment or any runway change is not issued before 10 NM from the runway-transition waypoint, radar vectors to final must be provided.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: If the runway assignment is not in place before 10 NM from the runway-transition waypoint, vectors to final are required.

items.question_text: Which arrival transmission correctly includes both the STAR and transition?

choices.text: Rosewood One arrival, Delta transition.
Proceed direct Delta, Rosewood only.
Arrival Rosewood, runway assigned later.
Delta transition, expect vectors at destination.

items.explanation: The approved phraseology states the STAR name and, when needed, the transition.

items.situation: The aircraft is cleared via the Rosewood One arrival, Delta transition. Build the arrival phraseology.
items.explanation: The correct transmission is "ROSEWOOD ONE ARRIVAL, DELTA TRANSITION."

items.situation: A STAR runway transition serves multiple runways, and the intended runway has still not been issued as the aircraft closes within 10 NM of the runway-transition waypoint.
items.para_context: When a STAR or RNAV STAR transition serves multiple runways, the intended runway must be stated on initial contact or as soon as practical. If the runway assignment or any subsequent runway change is not issued before 10 NM from the runway-transition waypoint, radar vectors to final must be provided.

choices.text: Provide radar vectors to final.
Allow the pilot to continue on the STAR without a runway assignment.
Issue only the altimeter and wait until the FAF.
Cancel the STAR and clear direct the airport.

items.explanation: Without the runway assignment by 10 NM from the transition waypoint, vectors to final are required.

items.question_text: What must you do before issuing a lower altitude to an aircraft landing at an airport near the common boundary in the adjacent center's area?

choices.text: Coordinate with the receiving facility for the lower altitude.
Issue the lower altitude first, then advise the receiving facility later.
Wait until the aircraft reports the airport in sight.
Use pilot's discretion in place of coordination.

items.explanation: Advance descent clearance near the boundary requires prior coordination with the receiving facility.

items.question_text: You must initiate the advance-descent action far enough from destination to allow for normal descent and speed reduction.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The action should be started at a distance sufficient for normal descent and speed reduction.

items.question_text: When you are controlling an aircraft landing at an airport in an adjacent center's area near the common boundary, you must coordinate for the lower altitude before issuing it.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That is the core coordination rule in advance descent clearance.

items.situation: An aircraft is descending toward an airport just inside the adjacent center's area, and you want to start it down early so it can make a normal descent profile.
items.para_context: When controlling an aircraft landing at an airport near the common boundary in an adjacent center's control area, coordinate with the receiving facility for the lower altitude and initiate the action at a distance sufficient for normal descent and speed reduction.

choices.text: Coordinate the lower altitude with the receiving facility, then issue the descent in time for a normal profile.
Issue the descent immediately without coordination because the destination is close to the boundary.
Keep the aircraft high until the adjacent center takes control, regardless of the descent profile.
Cancel the approach and tell the pilot to request descent from the next center.

items.explanation: Advance descent near the boundary requires both coordination and enough distance for a normal descent and speed reduction.

items.question_text: Under an authorized SFA procedure, when do you normally avoid requiring a radio-frequency change?

choices.text: After the aircraft begins approach, or after initial contact during an en route descent, until a landing or low approach has been completed.
Only after the aircraft reports the runway in sight.
Only below 2,500 feet AGL.
Only when the weather is IFR at the airport.

items.explanation: SFA procedures minimize frequency changes after approach begins or after initial contact during en route descent until landing or low approach completion.

items.question_text: When SFA procedures are authorized by letter of agreement, do not require a frequency change after the aircraft begins approach except under the listed conditions.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That is the core SFA restriction on radio-frequency changes.

items.question_text: If the pilot cancels the IFR flight plan, you may require the frequency change even though the aircraft was under an SFA procedure.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Pilot cancellation of IFR is one of the listed exceptions to the no-frequency-change rule.

items.question_text: This no-frequency-change restriction does not apply when the aircraft is cleared for a visual approach.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: A visual approach is one of the listed exceptions to the no-frequency-change rule.

items.question_text: For military single-piloted turbojet aircraft in IFR conditions or at night, what is the basic rule on radio-frequency and radar-beacon changes?

choices.text: Avoid them to the maximum extent communications capabilities and traffic will permit.
Make them at every sector boundary to standardize procedures.
Delay all changes until after touchdown.
Require a change at the approach fix in every case.

items.explanation: The paragraph directs controllers to avoid those changes to the maximum extent traffic and communications permit.

items.question_text: If a frequency or beacon change is required, give the instruction early enough for the aircraft to make the change before the approach fix or handoff point.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Required changes should be issued early enough to be completed before the approach fix or handoff point.

items.question_text: If a nonradar approach will be made, you may instruct the aircraft to change to tower frequency when the reported ceiling is at or above 1,500 feet and visibility is 5 statute miles or more.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That ceiling and visibility combination is one of the listed conditions that permits the tower-frequency change.

items.instruction: Which condition allows the tower-frequency change on a nonradar approach?
items.question_text: Which condition is specifically listed?

choices.text: The aircraft reports able to proceed by visual reference to the surface.
The aircraft is above 2,500 feet above the surface.
The approach controller wants to reduce workload.
The aircraft has entered the final approach course.

items.explanation: Reporting the ability to proceed by visual reference to the surface is one of the specific conditions for the tower-frequency change.

items.situation: A military single-piloted turbojet aircraft is making a turn in IFR conditions on approach, and a routine frequency change would normally be issued at that moment.
items.para_context: When military single-piloted turbojet aircraft conduct approaches wholly or partly in IFR conditions or at night, avoid frequency and beacon changes to the maximum extent possible. If changes are required, avoid requiring them during the time the aircraft is making a turn.

choices.text: Avoid requiring the change during the turn.
Issue the change immediately because the aircraft is already established on approach.
Require both the frequency and beacon change during the turn to minimize later workload.
Cancel the approach and re-sequence the aircraft.

items.explanation: The paragraph specifically says to avoid requiring those changes while the aircraft is making a turn.

items.question_text: When may an en route descent be used in a nonradar environment for a military turbojet arrival?

choices.text: When radar capability exists that will permit vectors to the final approach course of a published high-altitude procedure or PAR/ASR, and other than normal vectoring delays are not anticipated.
Whenever the pilot requests a high altitude approach, regardless of radar capability.
Only if no approach type has yet been chosen.
Only at airports with ATIS.

items.explanation: Nonradar en route descent for military turbojets still requires radar capability for vectors to final and no abnormal vectoring delays.

items.question_text: Before issuing a descent clearance below the highest initial-approach-fix altitude for any high-altitude instrument approach at the destination, you must tell the aircraft the type of approach to expect and that radar vectors will be provided to final approach course.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Those items must be given before the descent below the highest IAF altitude.

items.instruction: Is this ATIS omission rule correct?
items.question_text: If the pilot advises receipt of the current ATIS before the descent briefing is issued, you may omit the descent-briefing items that are already contained in that broadcast.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Items already contained in the ATIS may be omitted once the pilot confirms receipt of the current broadcast.

items.question_text: To avoid excessive low-altitude flight, the descent point is found by adding 10 to the first two digits of the flight level.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph states that the descent point is determined by adding 10 to the first two digits of the flight level.

items.instruction: Which weather briefing trigger is correct?
items.question_text: When must current weather be included in the military turbojet descent briefing?

choices.text: When the ceiling is below 1,000 feet, or USAF 1,500 feet, or below the highest circling minimum, whichever is greater, or the visibility is less than 3 miles.
Only when the visibility is below 1 mile.
Only when the pilot has not received ATIS and the ceiling is below 3,000 feet.
Whenever the airport has a circling minimum published, regardless of weather.

items.explanation: Those are the weather triggers that require current weather in the descent briefing.

items.situation: A military turbojet is at FL370 and requests normal arrival handling. You are planning the en route descent point.
items.para_context: To avoid requiring an aircraft to fly at low altitudes for an excessive distance, the descent point for a military turbojet en route descent is determined by adding 10 to the first two digits of the flight level. For FL370, that produces 47 miles.

choices.text: Plan the descent point at about 47 miles from the airport.
Plan the descent point at 37 miles from the airport.
Plan the descent point at 57 miles from the airport in every case.
Keep the aircraft level until the high-altitude approach fix regardless of distance.

items.explanation: For FL370, the rule produces 47 miles.

items.question_text: What information is forwarded to a nonapproach control tower or, in Alaska, to FSS for arriving aircraft?

choices.text: Aircraft identification, type, ETA, and the type of approach or equivalent arrival details.
Only the runway assignment and braking action.
Only the pilot's requested approach.
Only the latest ATIS code.

items.explanation: Arrival information forwarded to tower or FSS includes identification, type, ETA, and approach or equivalent details.

items.question_text: When forwarding arrival information to approach control, the ETA need not be given if the arrival information is being forwarded during a radar handoff.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The ETA is not required when the arrival information is sent during a radar handoff.

items.instruction: Which item belongs in the handoff package?
items.question_text: What must be forwarded to the approach control facility before transfer of control jurisdiction?

choices.text: Aircraft identification.
Current taxiway closures at destination.
ATIS code readback from the pilot.
Only destination gate information.

items.explanation: Aircraft identification is one of the required items forwarded before transfer of jurisdiction.

items.situation: You are forwarding arrival information to approach control before transfer of jurisdiction, and the clearance limit is not the destination airport.
items.para_context: Before transfer of control jurisdiction, en route controllers forward aircraft identification, type and equipment suffix, ETA or actual time and altitude over the clearance limit, the clearance limit when other than the destination airport plus any EFC, and the time, fix, or altitude when control responsibility transfers, unless an LOA omits those items.

choices.text: Include the clearance limit and any EFC that was issued.
Omit the clearance limit because approach control can infer it from the destination.
Send only the aircraft type and wait for approach to ask for the rest.
Forward the runway assignment instead of the clearance limit.

items.explanation: When the clearance limit is not the destination airport, that limit and any EFC issued are part of the required arrival information.

items.question_text: How early should radio communications and control responsibility be transferred?

choices.text: Early enough to allow the receiving facility to clear the aircraft beyond the clearance limit before it reaches that limit.
Only after the aircraft reaches the clearance limit.
At the final approach fix in every case.
Only after approach requests the transfer.

items.explanation: The transfer must occur early enough for the receiving facility to issue onward clearance before the aircraft reaches the limit.

items.question_text: Radio communications and control responsibility must be transferred early enough to allow the receiving facility to clear an aircraft beyond the clearance limit before the aircraft reaches it.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That is the direct rule in the paragraph.

items.situation: An aircraft is nearing its clearance limit, and the next facility still needs time to issue clearance beyond that limit.
items.para_context: Transfer radio communications and control responsibility early enough to allow the receiving facility to clear an aircraft beyond the clearance limit before the aircraft reaches it.

choices.text: Transfer earlier so the receiving facility can issue the onward clearance before the aircraft reaches the limit.
Wait until the aircraft reaches the limit, then transfer it.
Keep the aircraft on frequency until it reports entering a hold.
Cancel IFR so the next facility does not need to issue onward clearance.

items.explanation: The transfer must happen early enough for the receiving facility to act before the aircraft reaches the clearance limit.

items.question_text: When a STAR or RNAV STAR is assigned, what must the clearance include?
items.explanation: The clearance includes the STAR name and the transition if one is necessary.

choices.text: The STAR name and the transition, if necessary.
Only the destination airport.
Only the runway number.
Only the EFC time.

items.question_text: If the runway assignment or a later runway change is not issued before 10 NM from the runway-transition waypoint, radar vectors to final must be provided.
items.explanation: That is the stated runway-transition protection rule.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Which arrival transmission correctly includes both the STAR and transition?
items.explanation: The approved phraseology states the STAR name and, when needed, the transition.

choices.text: Rosewood One arrival, Delta transition.
Proceed direct Delta, Rosewood only.
Arrival Rosewood, runway assigned later.
Delta transition, expect vectors at destination.

items.question_text: When a STAR runway transition serves multiple runways, when must the intended runway number be stated?
items.explanation: The intended runway must be stated on initial contact or as soon as practical.

choices.text: On initial contact or as soon as practical.
Only after the aircraft reaches the runway-transition waypoint.
Only if the pilot asks for it.
No later than the FAF.

items.question_text: Which altitude option may be part of an arrival clearance?
items.explanation: Altitude information can be an assigned altitude or a vertical-navigation instruction on the STAR or transition.

choices.text: An assigned altitude or an instruction to vertically navigate on the STAR or STAR transition.
Only an EFC time.
Only a missed-approach instruction.
Only a runway exit instruction.

items.question_text: What must be done before issuing a lower altitude under an advance descent clearance near an adjacent center boundary?
items.explanation: The lower altitude must be coordinated with the receiving facility first.

choices.text: Coordinate the lower altitude with the receiving facility.
Issue the lower altitude first and coordinate later.
Wait until the pilot reports the airport in sight.
Issue pilot's discretion in place of coordination.

items.question_text: Advance descent action should be initiated far enough from destination to allow normal descent and speed reduction.
items.explanation: That is the stated timing standard.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Which aircraft is covered by this advance descent rule?
items.explanation: The rule applies when controlling aircraft landing at an airport in an adjacent center's control area near the common boundary.

choices.text: An aircraft landing at an airport in an adjacent center's area near the common boundary.
Any aircraft requesting a cruise clearance.
Only VFR aircraft approaching a Class D airport.
Only aircraft already on a published approach procedure.

items.question_text: Why is the action started sufficiently far from destination?
items.explanation: The distance is to permit normal descent and speed reduction.

choices.text: To allow normal descent and speed reduction.
To avoid all coordination with the receiving facility.
To force the pilot to request a higher speed.
To ensure the aircraft remains above the MEA until the FAF.

items.question_text: Under an authorized SFA procedure, when is a radio-frequency change normally avoided?
items.explanation: It is normally avoided after the aircraft begins approach or after initial contact during an en route descent until landing or low approach is completed.

choices.text: After approach begins or after initial contact during en route descent, until a landing or low approach is completed.
Only below 2,500 feet above the surface.
Only at night.
Only after the runway is in sight.

items.question_text: Pilot cancellation of the IFR flight plan is one of the exceptions to the no-frequency-change rule for SFA operations.
items.explanation: Cancellation of the IFR flight plan is one of the listed exceptions.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Which condition is a stated exception to the no-frequency-change rule for SFA operations?
items.explanation: Clearing the aircraft for a visual approach is one of the stated exceptions.

choices.text: The aircraft is cleared for a visual approach.
The aircraft is below 2,500 feet AGL.
The airport has multiple runways.
The aircraft is inside the FAF.

items.question_text: SFA procedures apply to what kind of aircraft and operation?
items.explanation: They apply to military single-piloted turbojet aircraft on an IFR flight plan where procedures are contained in a letter of agreement.

choices.text: Military single-piloted turbojet aircraft on an IFR flight plan under a letter-of-agreement procedure.
Any military aircraft on a VFR flight plan.
Any civil turbojet flying a STAR.
Only helicopters on contact approaches.

items.question_text: For military single-piloted turbojet aircraft in IFR conditions or at night, what is the general rule on frequency and beacon changes?
items.explanation: The changes should be avoided to the maximum extent communications capabilities and traffic will permit.

choices.text: Avoid them to the maximum extent communications capabilities and traffic will permit.
Make them at every sector boundary.
Require them at the approach fix in every case.
Delay all of them until after landing.

items.question_text: If a frequency or beacon change is required, it should be issued early enough to be completed before the approach fix or handoff point.
items.explanation: Required changes are issued early enough to be completed before the approach fix or handoff point.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Which condition allows a change to tower frequency for a nonradar approach?
items.explanation: One of the allowed conditions is that the aircraft reports able to proceed by visual reference to the surface.

choices.text: The aircraft reports able to proceed by visual reference to the surface.
The aircraft is merely inside the final approach fix.
The controller prefers fewer frequencies in use.
The aircraft has reached missed-approach altitude.

items.question_text: When should frequency or beacon changes be avoided during the approach?
items.explanation: They should be avoided while the aircraft is making a turn.

choices.text: During the time the aircraft is making a turn.
Only above the transition altitude.
Only after touchdown.
Only before the handoff point.

items.question_text: After a missed approach, when should a frequency or beacon change be withheld?
items.explanation: Do not require the change before the aircraft reaches the missed approach altitude, MEA, or MVA.

choices.text: Before the aircraft reaches the missed approach altitude, MEA, or MVA.
Only until the aircraft passes the outer marker.
Only until the pilot reports positive climb.
Only until the runway environment is out of sight.

items.question_text: When may an en route descent be used in a nonradar environment for a military turbojet arrival?
items.explanation: It may be used only when radar capability exists for vectors to final and other than normal vectoring delays are not anticipated.

choices.text: When radar capability exists for vectors to final and no abnormal vectoring delays are expected.
Whenever the pilot requests a high altitude approach, regardless of radar capability.
Only if ATIS is available.
Only in radar environments.

items.question_text: Before descent below the highest initial-approach-fix altitude for any high-altitude approach at the destination, what two items must always be included in the briefing?
items.explanation: The aircraft must be told the type of approach to expect and that radar vectors will be provided to final approach course.

choices.text: The type of approach to expect and that radar vectors will be provided to final approach course.
Only the missed approach and runway exit.
Only the descent point and holding fix.
Only the ATIS code and tower frequency.

items.question_text: If the pilot has received the current ATIS before the required descent briefing, items already contained in the ATIS may be omitted.
items.explanation: Items already contained in the ATIS may be omitted once the pilot confirms receipt of the current broadcast.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: How is the military turbojet descent point determined?
items.explanation: Add 10 to the first two digits of the flight level.

choices.text: Add 10 to the first two digits of the flight level.
Subtract 10 from the first two digits of the flight level.
Double the first two digits of the flight level.
Use the final approach fix distance only.

items.question_text: For FL370, what descent-point mileage does the rule produce?
items.explanation: For FL370, 37 plus 10 equals 47 miles.

choices.text: 47 miles.
37 miles.
57 miles.
27 miles.

items.question_text: May ATC terminate a military turbojet en route descent without pilot consent in normal operations?
items.explanation: No. ATC does not terminate the en route descent without pilot consent except for radar outage or emergency.

choices.text: False
True

items.question_text: What information is forwarded to a nonapproach control tower or, in Alaska, to FSS for arriving aircraft?
items.explanation: The forwarded arrival information includes identification, type, ETA, and approach or equivalent details.

choices.text: Aircraft identification, type, ETA, and the type of approach or equivalent arrival details.
Only runway assignment and braking action.
Only pilot requests and ATIS code.
Only destination gate information.

items.question_text: When forwarding arrival information during a radar handoff, the ETA may be omitted.
items.explanation: The ETA need not be given when arrival information is being forwarded during a radar handoff.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Which item must be forwarded to approach control before transfer of control jurisdiction?
items.explanation: Aircraft identification is one of the required items.

choices.text: Aircraft identification.
Current taxiway closures.
Only the ATIS code readback.
Only gate information.

items.question_text: If the clearance limit is other than the destination airport, what additional item is normally forwarded to approach control?
items.explanation: The clearance limit and any EFC issued are forwarded when the limit is other than the destination airport.

choices.text: The clearance limit and any EFC issued.
Only the destination gate.
Only the runway in use.
Only the current altimeter setting.

items.question_text: How early should communications and control responsibility be transferred?
items.explanation: The transfer must be early enough for the receiving facility to issue onward clearance before the aircraft reaches the clearance limit.

choices.text: Early enough for the receiving facility to clear the aircraft beyond the clearance limit before it reaches it.
Only after the aircraft reaches the clearance limit.
At the final approach fix in every case.
Only after the next facility specifically asks for the transfer.

items.question_text: Communications and control responsibility must be transferred early enough to let the receiving facility clear the aircraft beyond the clearance limit before it reaches that limit.
items.explanation: That is the direct rule in the paragraph.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Why is the transfer made early?
items.explanation: The purpose is to give the receiving facility enough time to issue onward clearance before the aircraft reaches the clearance limit.

choices.text: To let the receiving facility issue onward clearance before the aircraft reaches the clearance limit.
To ensure the pilot changes frequencies at the FAF.
To avoid issuing any further clearances.
To guarantee the aircraft enters holding at the limit.
